title: Investigating the Role of AI in Emergency Management
text:
COMPANION // FAccT ’26


Use cases, challenges, and opportunities — turned into a project picker and onboarding kit for computing researchers.

32 interviewed emergency managers ·  7 use-case categories ·  136 specific use cases

Open the catalog → Read the paper

title: Investigating the Role of AI in Emergency Management
text:
Start here

 01 / CATALOG

Use-Case Catalog


All 136 use cases, filterable by category, EM phase, feasibility, and current AI use — each with an “open problems” column.

  02 / DATA

Datasets Starter Pack


xBD, HumAID, CrisisBench, CrisisLex, CrisisTransformers — with their known geographic and linguistic biases noted up front.

  03 / DOCTRINE

Domain Literacy


NIMS/ICS, the Whole Community doctrine, EMAP standards, a sample ICS-201/IAP, and the staffing + funding evidence base.

  04 / PEOPLE

Meet an EM


How to engage practitioners ethically: where to find them, what a ride-along or tabletop involves, and the norms that matter.

  05 / PITFALLS

Anti-Patterns


The rabbit holes — activation prediction, autonomous life-safety decisions, misinformation-detection overpromises — and why.

  06 / METHOD

Reusable Instruments


The interview protocol and codebook, offered for replication — including the international extension the paper invites.

title: Investigating the Role of AI in Emergency Management
text:
The four phases
Emergency management runs on a cyclical four-phase model. Throughout this companion, phase chips mark which part of the cycle a use case touches.

Mitigation Preparedness Response Recovery







TipHow to cite



Foubert, S., Park, M., Pardeshi, K. S., Manzini, T., McDaniel, R., Merrick, D., Murphy, R., Singh, A., & Heidari, H. (2026). Investigating the Role of AI in Emergency Management: Use Cases, Challenges, and Opportunities. FAccT ’26. https://doi.org/10.1145/3805689.3812279

title: Anti-Patterns
text: These are not “hard problems worth tackling.” They are problems where the binding constraint is not AI capability, so an AI-first approach predictably fails. Read this before scoping a project.

title: Anti-Patterns
text:
1. EOC activation prediction
Building a model that decides when to activate an Emergency Operations Center.
Why it’s a trap: there are ~1,400 counties in the U.S., and essentially every one has different activation criteria. Activation decisions turn on liability, elected-official preferences, political pressure, resource availability, and community expectations — factors that don’t reduce to algorithmic rules. Any tool is either too rigid to fit local variation or too vague to be actionable.

title: Anti-Patterns
text:
2. Autonomous decision systems for life-safety
Systems that decide and act without a human in the loop.
Why it’s a trap: participants were unanimous — EM AI must preserve human decision-making authority. Acceptable autonomy tops out at AI suggests, human approves (Levels 1–2). No one wanted autonomous action for life-safety, because the human remains legally and politically accountable regardless of what the system recommended. Design for the operator and the collaborator, not the autonomous agent.

title: Anti-Patterns
text:
3. Misinformation-detection overpromises
Real-time AI that detects and flags false information during a crisis.
Why it’s a trap: misinformation spreads faster than verification is possible, classifier accuracy drops sharply on novel claims, and false positives risk suppressing legitimate community-sourced intelligence. Ground truth itself is often uncertain during an active event. Promise message drafting (tractable); be very cautious about misinfo detection (not).

title: Anti-Patterns
text:
4. Adding data streams without synthesis
“Let’s give them one more dashboard / feed / alert source.”
Why it’s a trap: the core complaint is drowning in data without synthesis. Adding inputs without filtering, prioritizing, and summarizing makes the fundamental problem worse. Reduce cognitive load or don’t ship.

title: Anti-Patterns
text:
5. Assuming reliable infrastructure
Cloud-dependent tools that need power, cell, and internet.
Why it’s a trap: response and recovery happen precisely when infrastructure fails. A tool that’s unavailable during the disaster is unavailable when it matters. Design and test for degraded conditions — intermittent connectivity, partial data, stressed users.

title: Anti-Patterns
section: 6. Treating EMs as data sources, not partners
text:
6. Treating EMs as data sources, not partners
Extracting labels and requirements, then disappearing.
Why it’s a trap: it risks deskilling the very expertise the system depends on, and it produces tools that fail on contact with real workflows. Bring practitioners into design, not just data collection.






TipThe pattern behind the patterns



Before scoping, ask: is the binding constraint here AI capability, or is it interoperability, accountability, infrastructure, or institutions? If it’s the latter, no model fixes it — and that’s worth knowing on day one.

title: Meet an EM
text: The fastest way to build the wrong tool is to never talk to an emergency manager. The second fastest is to talk to them badly. This page is about doing it well.

title: Meet an EM
text:
Where to find them

IAEM — the International Association of Emergency Managers hosts an annual conference (with AI workshops) and regional chapters. A good first point of contact. TODO: link.
State EM associations — most states have one; they often maintain professional distribution lists. These were the backbone of recruitment for this study.
Your university’s EM connections — campus emergency management, public-health, or robotics groups frequently have practitioner ties.
LinkedIn + snowball referrals — practical and how much of this study’s sample was reached; ask each contact to refer one more.

title: Meet an EM
section: Ways to observe (lowest to highest commitment)
text:
Ways to observe (lowest to highest commitment)

A conversation / interview — 60–90 minutes. Use scenario-based prompts: ask them to walk through a specific incident they handled, then ask where technology could have helped. Concrete beats abstract every time.
An EOC visit — see the Emergency Operations Center and the tools (WebEOC, GIS) in their actual context.
A tabletop exercise — observe a simulated scenario. Exercises exist “because that’s the perfect time to make mistakes,” which also makes them a safe place to watch decision-making unfold.
A ride-along — the deepest immersion; arrange only through an established relationship.

title: Meet an EM
text:
Norms that matter






WarningCome during blue sky, never during an activation



EMs spend ~95% of their time in non-activated “blue sky” periods. That is when they have time for you. Reaching out during an active disaster is, at best, ignored — and at worst, an interruption to life-safety work. Time it accordingly.



Their time is scarce and often unpaid-adjacent. Many are departments of one. Be brief, be prepared, and make the value exchange explicit.
Ground questions in their work, not your tool. Start with their job and a real decision they made before introducing AI. This yields ecologically valid insight instead of speculation.
They are accountable for outcomes. “The AI told me to” is not a defense they can use in front of county commissioners. Respect that explainability and human authority are non-negotiable for them.
Get IRB approval for anything resembling research. This study ran under exempt status (Category 2: interviews and observation of public behavior). TODO: link your IRB resources.

title: Use-Case Catalog
Datasets Starter Pack
text: Start here instead of from scratch. Each of these is mature enough to build on, and each carries a known limitation that will quietly shape your results if you ignore it. The point of this page is to start with eyes open.

title: Datasets Starter Pack
text:
Damage assessment (computer vision)
xBD — the anchor benchmark for building-damage assessment from satellite imagery: over 850,000 building annotations across roughly 45,000 km² spanning six disaster types.

⚠️ Known bias: damage-assessment models show reduced performance on Global South contexts. Evaluate across geographic subgroups before claiming generality.


xBD: A Dataset for Assessing Building Damage from Satellite Imagery (Gupta et al., 2019) Paper
Related:

U-Net: Convolutional Networks for Biomedical Image Segmentation (Ronneberger et al., 2015) Paper
Large-scale Building Damage Assessment using a Novel Hierarchical Transformer Architecture on Satellite Images (Kaur et al., 2023) Paper

title: Datasets Starter Pack
text:
Crisis NLP (text classification)

HumAID: Human-Annotated Disaster Incidents Data from Twitter with Deep Learning Benchmarks (Alam et al., 2021) Paper Dataset
CrisisBench: Benchmarking Crisis-related Social Media Datasets for Humanitarian Information Processing (Alam et al. 2021) Paper Dataset
CrisisLex: A Lexicon for Collecting and Filtering Microblogged Communications in Crises (Olteanu et al., 2014) Paper Website





⚠️ Known bias: crisis NLP corpora are predominantly English-centric. Performance for non-English and code-switched messages is largely unmeasured.

title: Datasets Starter Pack
text:
Domain-specific language models
CrisisTransformers — pretrained language models and sentence encoders for crisis-related text; reported to outperform general-purpose models by roughly 10–17% on crisis-classification tasks (Lamsal et al., 2024).
CrisisTransformers: Pre-trained language models and sentence encoders for crisis-related social media texts (Lamsal et al., 2024) Paper Models

Useful when general LLMs underperform on short, noisy, disaster-specific text.

title: Datasets Starter Pack
text:
Search and rescue (aerial imagery)
Deep-learning approaches to aerial-imagery analysis and reinforcement-learning search-pattern optimization are technically well established. Some examples: - Deep Learning Approach in Aerial Imagery for Supporting Land Search and Rescue Missions (Božić-Štulić et al., 2019) Paper - UAV-Based Real-Time Survivor Detection System in Post-Disaster Search and Rescue Operations (Dong et al., 2021) Paper - Deep reinforcement learning for time-critical wilderness search and rescue using drones (Ewers et al., 2025) Paper

⚠️ Known bias: publicly available SAR imagery datasets are geographically limited to European terrain. Models may not transfer to other landscapes.







ImportantThe cross-cutting lesson



Training-data biases — the geographic limits of damage datasets, the English-centricity of crisis NLP — risk creating systems that perform worst for already-marginalized communities. Treat subgroup evaluation as a default step, not an afterthought.


Full citations are in the paper’s reference list. TODO: link each dataset to its repository / download page.

title: Reusable Instruments
text: The study deliberately focused on U.S. emergency managers operating under U.S. doctrine (NIMS, ICS, FEMA). International EM runs on different frameworks — for example, the UN cluster approach — and may surface different requirements. These instruments are shared so others can run the extension.

title: Reusable Instruments
text:
What’s here

Interview protocol — the full semi-structured script (background → decision scenarios → resource-constrained scenarios → protective-action and evacuation scenarios → improvement opportunities → recovery-center planning).
Pre-interview questionnaire — AI-familiarity items adapted from the Pew Research Center’s survey, plus optional demographics.
Codebook (v3.0) — five top-level themes: Decision-Making Scenarios, Technology Use Cases, EM Context, Current Tech Usage, and Future Tech Imaginings.

title: Reusable Instruments
text:
Design choices worth reusing

Funnel structure — open with their background and a real disaster they handled; introduce technology and AI only later. Grounding participants in concrete experience before speculation yields more valid responses.
Critical-incident / scenario-based elicitation — ask participants to walk through a specific decision (process, data consulted, stakeholders, outcome) before asking evaluative questions about where AI could help.
Tailoring by AI familiarity — for participants who use AI at work, probe specifics; for those who don’t, focus on general technology experience and hypotheticals.
Reflexive thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke) — open coding generated 847 initial codes; iterative constant-comparison rounds synthesized them into the codebook and seven use-case categories.

title: Reusable Instruments
text:
Adapting internationally
If you’re replicating outside the U.S.:

Swap the doctrine-specific references (NIMS/ICS/FEMA, ICS form numbers, EMAP) for your jurisdiction’s equivalents.
Re-examine the recovery-center section (Disaster Recovery Centers / MARCs are U.S. constructs).
Keep the funnel and critical-incident techniques — they’re framework-agnostic.
Report your sample’s AI familiarity with the same validated items to enable cross-study comparison.

If you run an extension, the authors would value hearing about it — open an issue at https://github.com/samsaranc/ai-for-emergency-management/issues.

title: Domain Literacy
text: You don’t need to become an emergency manager. You do need to recognize the frameworks they live inside, because those frameworks determine what a deployable system even looks like.

title: Domain Literacy
text:
Operating doctrine

NIMS / ICS — the National Incident Management System and Incident Command System are the standardized frameworks (mandated by presidential directive after 9/11) that structure how personnel, resources, and information flow during an incident. The same structures scale from local flooding to multi-state disasters. National Incident Management System, 3rd ed. (DHS/FEMA, 2017). Link
The Whole Community approach — the doctrine of genuinely engaging community members, especially those most vulnerable to disaster impacts, across all four phases. It emerged from documented disparities in disaster outcomes (e.g., Hurricane Katrina). This is why equity is not an add-on in EM — it’s foundational. A Whole Community Approach to Emergency Management (FEMA, 2011). Link

title: Domain Literacy
text:
Standards and forms

EMAP — the Emergency Management Accreditation Program provides the standardized criteria plans are evaluated against ANSI/EMAP 5-2022. This matters for AI: it gives you objective ground truth for any plan-analysis tool.
ICS forms — EM is heavily text-driven, with standardized forms (ICS-201 through ICS-215) governing information flow. The ICS-201 (Incident Briefing) and the Incident Action Plan (IAP) are the ones you’ll hear about most.

ICS forms
Sample ICS-201
Sample IAP template

title: Domain Literacy
text:
Why this matters (the evidence base)
The field is chronically under-resourced — which is exactly why pragmatic, imperfect tools are welcome here.



Finding
Source




57% of local EM agencies run on one or fewer full-time employees
Argonne / NPAC staffing study, 2025 Link


56% of directors are “dual-hatting” (EM is not their only job)
Argonne / NPAC, 2025 Link


15% of local director positions are unpaid volunteers
Argonne / NPAC, 2025 Link


State EM budgets range $500K–$530M; median under $10M
Deloitte–NEMA National Risk Study, 2025 Link


Agencies want AI but call commercial offerings “not quite there yet”
Deloitte–NEMA, 2025 Link


Burnout exceeds 50% among emergency-services personnel
Patel et al., 2023 Link









Note



The takeaway from this table is not “EM is sad.” It’s that the bar for useful is low and the appetite is high — a domain where a “good enough” tool that saves real hours is genuinely valued.
